tasks.title: Extend FormattedItemEditState with pending_cursor_pos field
tasks.description: Add a field to store the computed raw text cursor position when clicking in display mode.
tasks.details: In src/markdown/editor.rs, add `pending_cursor_pos: Option<usize>` to the FormattedItemEditState struct. This will hold the raw text index computed from the display click position. Initialize as None in the struct's default or new method.
tasks.testStrategy: Verify struct compiles and can be instantiated with pending_cursor_pos set to Some(5) and None values. Check serialization/deserialization if applicable.

tasks.title: Implement displayed text extraction function
tasks.description: Create a function to generate the displayed (rendered) text from raw markdown by stripping formatting markers.
tasks.details: Write a utility function `fn get_displayed_text(raw_text: &str) -> String` that parses the raw markdown and returns text without markers like **, *, `, [](). Use simple regex or manual parsing for inline formats: bold(**), italic(*), code(`), links([text](url)). Skip block-level parsing since single-line focus.
tasks.testStrategy: Unit tests: Input `- **bold** *italic* `code` [link](url)` → Output `- bold italic code link`. Test edge cases: empty string, plain text, malformed markers.

tasks.title: Implement click-to-displayed-index mapping using egui Galley
tasks.description: On mouse click in display mode, use egui's Galley to map screen click position to character index in displayed text.
tasks.details: In render_paragraph_sk() and render_list_item() click handlers: Capture click_pos in screen coordinates relative to text_rect. Create galley: `let galley = ui.painter().layout_no_wrap(&displayed_text, font_id, color);`. Compute `let cursor = galley.cursor_from_pos(click_pos - text_rect.min); let displayed_idx = cursor.ccursor.index;`. Store displayed_idx temporarily.
tasks.testStrategy: Mock egui context, test galley creation doesn't panic, verify cursor_from_pos returns index 0- len(displayed_text) for various positions. Integration test in UI.

tasks.title: Build raw-to-displayed position mapping and compute raw cursor position
tasks.description: Create bidirectional mapping between raw and displayed text positions to convert displayed index to raw index.
tasks.details: Implement `fn map_displayed_to_raw(displayed_idx: usize, raw_text: &str, displayed_text: &str) -> usize`. Walk parallel: track raw_pos and displayed_pos, skip raw formatting markers (e.g., ** pairs, * pairs, ` pairs, [text](url) structures). When displayed_pos >= displayed_idx, return current raw_pos. Handle links by skipping entire [text](url) in raw.
tasks.testStrategy: Unit tests with table examples: e.g., raw `*h**e**re*` displayed `here`, displayed_idx=2 → raw_idx=8 (position of 'r'). Test all formats: bold, italic, code, links. Verify monotonic increasing mapping.

tasks.title: Integrate cursor mapping into edit mode entry and update render functions
tasks.description: Connect all pieces: on click store pending_cursor_pos, apply it when entering edit mode in both render_paragraph_sk and render_list_item.
tasks.details: In both render_paragraph_sk() (2 locations) and render_list_item() (2 locations) in src/markdown/editor.rs: On click, compute displayed_text, displayed_idx (task3), raw_idx = map_displayed_to_raw(displayed_idx, raw, displayed_text) (task4), set state.pending_cursor_pos = Some(raw_idx). On edit mode enter: use existing TextEdit cursor setting code, passing pending_cursor_pos, then reset to None. Ensure works in rendered and split view modes.
tasks.testStrategy: Manual UI tests with PRD test cases: create file with `- This is **bold** text`, `- Click *here* to edit`, etc. Click various positions (start/mid/end of formats), verify cursor within 1-2 chars in edit mode. Test long lines (>100 chars). Performance: measure galley creation time <1ms.

tasks.title: Fix memory leak in viewer states by cleaning up HashMap entries on tab close
tasks.description: Implement cleanup of tree_viewer_states, csv_viewer_states, and sync_scroll_states HashMap entries when tabs are closed in the tab close handler in src/app.rs.
tasks.details: In src/app.rs, locate the tab close handler (likely in the main app update loop or TabManager::close_tab method). For the tab being closed, extract its unique identifier (tab_id: likely UUID or usize index). Then explicitly remove entries from all three global state HashMaps: `tree_viewer_states.remove(&tab_id);`, `csv_viewer_states.remove(&tab_id);`, `sync_scroll_states.remove(&tab_id);`. Ensure these HashMaps are mutable references (likely stored in app state: `self.tree_viewer_states.remove(...)`). Call `.shrink_to_fit()` or `.shrink_to(0)` after removal if memory pressure is high, but test performance impact first since Rust HashMap may not immediately release memory to OS[1][5]. Verify tab_id matches exactly the keys used when states were inserted (consistency check). Handle case where entry doesn't exist (HashMap::remove returns Option). Consider adding logging: `info!("Cleaned viewer states for tab {}", tab_id);`. If using Arc<Mutex<HashMap>> or RwLock, ensure proper locking scope.
tasks.testStrategy: Unit test: Mock HashMap with sample tab_ids, insert test data (Vec<u8> or structs simulating viewer state), call cleanup function, assert entry removed (hashmap.get(&tab_id) == None) and keys len decreased. Memory test: Use heaptrack/Valgrind or cargo-asm to verify no allocations persist post-cleanup[1][4]. Integration test: Open multiple tabs with tree/csv viewers, enable sync scroll, close tabs one-by-one, monitor RSS via `ps` or `htop` - confirm memory drops proportionally (no growth over 10+ open/close cycles). Manual test: Load large tree/csv files (1MB+), open 20 tabs, close all, verify app memory returns to baseline (<100MB). Test edge: Close non-existent tab_id (no panic), rapid open/close (100 tabs/sec).

tasks.title: Add custom memory allocator with mimalloc (Windows) and jemalloc (Linux/macOS)
tasks.description: Integrate platform-specific high-performance memory allocators into Cargo.toml and configure them as the global allocator in main.rs to reduce heap fragmentation and memory usage.
tasks.details:
1. Update Cargo.toml with platform-specific dependencies:
   - For Windows: `[dependencies] mimalloc = { version = "0.1", default-features = false }`
   - For Linux/macOS: `[dependencies] jemallocator = "0.5"`
   2. In src/main.rs, add conditional global allocator attribute before main() function:
   ```rust
   #[cfg(target_os = "windows")]
   #[global_allocator]
   static GLOBAL: mimalloc::MiMalloc = mimalloc::MiMalloc;

   #[cfg(all(unix, not(target_os = "macos")))]
   #[global_allocator]
   static GLOBAL: jemallocator::Jemalloc = jemallocator::Jemalloc;

   #[cfg(target_os = "macos")]
   #[global_allocator]
   static GLOBAL: jemallocator::Jemalloc = jemallocator::Jemalloc;
   ```
   3. Ensure no conflicts with existing allocators or FFI code. Add cargo-feature flags if needed for optional enabling: `[features] default = ["high-perf-alloc"]` and gate dependencies behind it. 4. Verify build succeeds on all target platforms (cross-compilation if needed). Consider musl targets separately with mimalloc due to performance issues with default musl allocator.
tasks.testStrategy: 1. Build and run on Windows: verify mimalloc active (`cargo run --target x86_64-pc-windows-msvc`), check no allocator conflicts. 2. Build and run on Linux/macOS: verify jemalloc active (`ldd target/debug/app | grep jemalloc` or `jemalloc stats print`). 3. Performance benchmarks: Use `cargo criterion` or `hyperfine` to measure allocation-heavy operations (tab creation/close from Task 6, markdown rendering from Tasks 2-5) before/after - target 10-20% RSS reduction and faster allocation times. 4. Memory leak verification: Run Task 6 tests with heaptrack/Valgrind, confirm no regressions. 5. Cross-platform CI: Add GitHub Actions matrix for windows-latest, ubuntu-latest, macos-latest with memory usage assertions.

tasks.title: Implement CJK font lazy loading with OnceCell
tasks.description: Implement on-demand loading of CJK fonts using OnceCell/lazy_static when CJK characters are detected in text, avoiding 50-100MB startup load for non-CJK users.
tasks.details: 1. In **src/fonts.rs**, create `static CJK_FONT: OnceCell<FontData> = OnceCell::new();` (or `lazy_static!` if preferred). Define CJK ranges as const arrays: `const CJK_RANGES: &[(u32,u32)] = &[(0x4E00,0x9FFF), (0x3040,0x309F), (0x30A0,0x30FF), (0xAC00,0xD7AF)];`. 2. Implement `fn needs_cjk(text: &str) -> bool`: iterate chars, check `c as u32` against ranges using binary search or simple range checks. 3. Implement `fn get_cjk_font() -> &'static FontData`: `CJK_FONT.get_or_init(|| load_cjk_font_from_bytes(include_bytes!("path/to/cjk-font.ttf")))`. Use existing font loading API. 4. In text rendering functions (render_paragraph_sk, render_list_item in src/markdown/editor.rs): before galley creation, check `if needs_cjk(&displayed_text) { cjk_font_id = get_cjk_font().font_id(); } else { cjk_font_id = default_font_id(); }`. Pass appropriate FontId to egui layout. 5. Update font selection logic to fallback to CJK font only when needed. Ensure thread-safe init with OnceCell. Handle font metrics consistency between default/CJK fonts.
tasks.testStrategy: 1. **Unit tests** for `needs_cjk()`: Test CJK chars (U+4E00 '一', U+3040 'が', U+AC00 '가') return true; ASCII/Latin return false; edge cases (U+9FFF, U+309F, U+D7AF). 2. **Integration tests**: Mock font loading, verify `get_cjk_font()` called only once per app run, subsequent calls return same instance. 3. **UI tests**: Create markdown with/without CJK: `- Hello world` (no load), `- 你好世界` (loads CJK). Monitor memory: startup <10MB without CJK, ~60MB after first CJK render. Use `cargo test` + manual profiling. 4. **Performance**: Time galley creation with/without CJK, ensure <5ms delta. Test in edit/display modes using existing cursor mapping.

tasks.title: Reduce undo history memory with size-based limits
tasks.description: Lower default max_undo_size from 100 to 50 and implement file size-based undo limits (20 entries for >1MB files, 10 for >5MB) to significantly reduce memory usage from full text snapshots.
tasks.details: 1. In the undo manager struct (likely in src/editor/undo.rs or src/app.rs), change the default `max_undo_size: usize = 50;` from 100. 2. Add `fn get_max_undo_size(file_path: &Path) -> usize`: read file size via `std::fs::metadata(file_path).map(|m| m.len()).unwrap_or(0)`, then return: if size > 5MB (5_000_000) → 10, else if >1MB (1_000_000) → 20, else 50. 3. Modify undo push logic: before `history.past.push(current_snapshot)`, call `let limit = get_max_undo_size(&active_file_path); if history.past.len() >= limit { history.past.remove(0); }` (using VecDeque or Vec::remove(0) with occasional shrink). 4. Update `history.undo()` and `history.redo()` to respect dynamic limits. 5. Store `active_file_path` in editor state or pass via context. 6. Add logging: `info!("Undo limit set to {} for file {} ({} bytes)", limit, file_path.display(), size);`. Ensure thread-safe if multi-threaded (use RwLock if needed). Consider using `VecDeque` for O(1) pop_front.
tasks.testStrategy: 1. **Unit tests**: Mock file metadata returning 500KB→assert limit=50, 2MB→20, 6MB→10. Test push beyond limit: create Vec with 60 dummy snapshots (each ~10KB String), push 5x, assert len=50 and first 10 dropped. 2. **Integration tests**: Open 1.5MB file, make 30 edits, verify undo stack ≤20 entries via debug print or exposed API. Open 500KB file, verify ≤50. 3. **Memory tests**: Use `cargo bloat` or heaptrack before/after: assert ~50% memory reduction for 2MB file with 100→20 limit (measure Vec<String> allocations). 4. **UI tests**: Edit large file, undo 25 steps, verify can't undo beyond limit, no panic/crash.

tasks.title: Implement egui temporary data cleanup on tab close
tasks.description: Clean up stale SyntaxHighlightCache and other per-tab temporary data stored in egui's ui.memory when tabs are closed, using ui.memory_mut() in the tab close handler to remove entries matching the closed tab's Id prefix.
tasks.details: 1. **Identify per-tab data in egui memory**: Locate SyntaxHighlightCache (likely from egui_commonmark or syntect integration) and other temp data (e.g. galley caches, layout jobs, TextEdit states) stored with tab-specific Ids. Common patterns: `Id::new("syntax_cache").with("tab_" + tab_id.to_string())` or `tab_id_prefix + "_highlight_cache"`. 2. **In src/app.rs tab close handler** (same location as Task 6 cleanup): Get `tab_id` or `tab_prefix` (e.g. `format!("tab_{}", tab_id)`). Access `ui.memory_mut()` and iterate/remove matching entries: ```rust let memory = ui.memory_mut(|m| m.clone()); for (id, _) in memory.data.iter() { if let Some(id_str) = id.as_str() { if id_str.starts_with(&tab_prefix) { ui.memory_mut(|m| m.data.remove(id)); } } } ``` 3. **Target specific caches**: Explicitly clear `SyntaxHighlightCache` if accessible via `ui.data_mut(|d| d.remove::<SyntaxHighlightCache>(tab_id))`. Handle egui_commonmark caches if used (CommonMarkCache per-tab instances). 4. **Edge cases**: Handle partial prefixes, nested Ids (`tab_123_paragraph_5`), concurrent closes. Use `ui.ctx().memory_mut()` if in different context. 5. **Verify no panics**: Test with empty memory, non-existent prefixes.
tasks.testStrategy: 1. **Unit tests**: Mock `egui::Memory` with sample tab data (`insert Id::new("tab_123_highlight"), insert Id::new("tab_123_galley_0"))`), call cleanup with prefix `"tab_123"`, assert only matching entries removed, unrelated data (`"tab_456_foo"`, `"global_cache"`) preserved. 2. **Integration tests**: Open 2 tabs with syntax-highlighted code blocks (using egui_commonmark/syntect), verify memory usage ~2x baseline. Close one tab, assert memory drops ~50%, second tab highlighting unchanged. 3. **Memory leak test**: Use heaptrack/Valgrind on tab open/close cycle (10 tabs × 3 opens/closes), verify no persistent allocations post-cleanup. 4. **UI tests**: Syntax highlighting in remaining tabs works post-close, no panics on rapid tab close/open. Monitor `ui.memory().data.len()` before/after.

tasks.title: Fix workspace close button alignment
tasks.description: Adjust the workspace panel close X button position 5-10px to the left to prevent overlap with resize handles and improve visual alignment.
tasks.details: 1. **Locate workspace panel UI code**: Search for workspace panel rendering in src/app.rs or src/workspace.rs (likely in a SidePanel::left('workspace') or similar, with `ui.horizontal(|ui| { ... Button::new("✕").clicked() ... })`). 2. **Identify close button**: Find the close button (typically `Button::new("×").fill([16.0,16.0]).clicked()` or `ui.close_button()`). 3. **Adjust position**: Wrap button in `ui.horizontal(|ui| { ui.add_space(5.0); // or 8.0 ui.add(close_button); })` OR use `ui.with_layout(Layout::right_to_left(Align::TOP), |ui| { ui.add_space(8.0); if ui.button("✕").clicked() { close_workspace(); } })`. 4. **Prevent resize handle overlap**: Ensure button rect doesn't intersect resize area by checking `ui.min_rect()` vs panel edge, add `min_size([20.0,20.0])` if needed. 5. **Visual verification**: Button should appear 5-10px from right edge, clear of resize drag zone. Alternative: Use `egui_alignments` crate if available (`Button::new("✕").right(ui)`).
tasks.testStrategy: 1. **Visual inspection**: Run app, open workspace panel, verify X button positioned 5-10px from right edge (measure with screenshot tools), no overlap with resize handle (drag test: resize should work without button interference). 2. **Interaction test**: Click close button multiple times, confirm workspace closes reliably, no phantom clicks on resize area. 3. **Resize test**: Drag resize handle left/right with button visible, ensure smooth resizing without button capture. 4. **Unit test** (if layout extractable): Mock Ui, measure button `response.rect.right()` vs `ui.available_rect_before_wrap().right()`, assert difference in 5.0..=10.0 range.

tasks.title: Fix new file dirty flag to skip save prompt for unmodified untitled files
tasks.description: Modify the file close/quit save prompt logic to check if the file is untitled AND content matches the initial empty state before prompting the user to save.
tasks.testStrategy: 1. **New file unmodified test**: Create new 'Untitled' tab, close without typing → verify **no** save prompt, tab closes cleanly. 2. **New file modified test**: Create new tab, type 'hello' → attempt close → verify save prompt appears with correct filename 'Untitled'. 3. **Saved file test**: Create new tab, save as 'test.txt', close → verify **no** prompt (distinguishes from untitled). 4. **Empty loaded file test**: Open existing empty file → close → verify **prompt** appears (not treated as new). 5. **Quit app test**: Open 2 new tabs (one modified, one clean), quit app → verify prompt only for modified tab, clean new tab closes silently. 6. **Rename test**: New tab → rename to 'foo.txt' → close unmodified → verify **prompt** (no longer 'new'). 7. **Integration**: Open 5 new tabs, modify 2, close all → verify prompts only for modified tabs.

tasks.title: Fix CJK first-line indentation to apply only to paragraph first line
tasks.description: Modify CJK paragraph indentation logic to apply text-indent only to the first line of each paragraph in the rendered view, rather than all lines.
tasks.details:
1. **Locate CJK indentation logic**: Search for current text-indent application in render_paragraph_sk() or equivalent rendering function (likely in src/render.rs or src/app.rs CentralPanel text rendering). Look for patterns like `text_style.visual_transform().indent` or `if is_cjk_paragraph { galley = layout_with_indent(text, 2.0 * em_size) }` applied to full paragraph text[1][2]. 2. **Identify paragraph boundaries**: Use needs_cjk() from Task 8 to detect CJK content. Split paragraph text by double newlines (`

`) or paragraph markers to isolate first line: `let lines: Vec<&str> = paragraph.split('

').collect(); let first_line = lines[0].split('
').next().unwrap_or(paragraph);`. 3. **Apply first-line-only indent**: Create galley for first line with indent: `let mut first_galley = ui.painter().layout_no_wrap(first_line, cjk_font_id, color); first_galley.galley_rect.min.x += 2.0 * font_size; // 2em CJK indent`. Render subsequent lines without indent using standard layout. 4. **Handle wrapping edge cases**: For wrapped first line, ensure indent applies only to first visual line (use galley.row_heights or cursor_from_pos logic from Task 3). 5. **CJK font integration**: Use get_cjk_font() from Task 8: `let font_id = if needs_cjk(first_line) { cjk_font_id } else { default_font_id };`. 6. **Preserve edit buffer**: Ensure source text remains unchanged (indent is render-only), no modifications to undo stack or TextEdit state.
tasks.testStrategy: 1. **Unit tests**: Test `needs_cjk()` integration with sample CJK paragraph ('第一段落文本...'), verify first line gets 2em indent (measure galley.rect.min.x), subsequent lines have 0 indent. Test mixed CJK/Latin: 'Hello 第一行 Second line' → only first line indented if CJK detected. 2. **Visual regression**: Create test file with 3 CJK paragraphs (each 3+ lines), render screenshot before/after, verify **only first line of each paragraph** indented ~2em (measure pixel distance), other lines flush left. 3. **Click mapping preservation**: Use Task 3 galley cursor_from_pos on indented first line, verify click positions correctly map to character indices despite indent offset. 4. **Performance**: Time render_paragraph_sk() with 100-line CJK doc before/after, ensure <5% slowdown. 5. **Edge cases**: Single-line CJK paragraph (should indent), empty first line, all-whitespace paragraph, mixed font paragraphs.

tasks.title: Add session restore settings option
tasks.description: Implement a restore_session boolean setting (default true) in app settings and add a toggle in the settings UI. When disabled, start the app with an empty tab instead of restoring the previous session.
tasks.details: 1. **Add setting to app state**: In src/app.rs or src/settings.rs, add `restore_session: bool` to the Settings struct with `#[serde(default = "true")]` for persistence. Update load/save logic: `settings.restore_session = settings_file.get("restore_session").unwrap_or(true);` and `settings_file.insert("restore_session", settings.restore_session);`. 2. **Modify app startup logic**: Locate session restore code (likely in `App::new()` or `CentralPanel` init: search for `if let Some(saved_session) = load_session() { tabs = saved_session.tabs; }`). Wrap in `if self.settings.restore_session { restore_session(); } else { self.tabs.clear(); self.tabs.push(new_empty_tab()); }`. Ensure `new_empty_tab()` creates standard untitled tab like Task 12 logic. 3. **Add UI toggle**: In settings panel (search for `SidePanel::right("settings")` or `if ui.button("Settings").clicked() { settings_open = true; }`), add `ui.checkbox(&mut self.settings.restore_session, "Restore previous session on startup");`. Use `ui.separator()` for grouping under 'General' or 'Startup' section. Style with `ui.label("When disabled, starts with empty tab.");`. 4. **Session persistence**: Ensure session save (on quit/close) uses similar path logic: `if self.settings.restore_session { save_session(&self.tabs); }`. Handle edge cases: first-run (no session file), corrupted session (fallback to empty), quit without save (no restore). 5. **File location**: Use consistent path like `~/.config/appname/session.json` matching existing settings file.
tasks.testStrategy: 1. **Default behavior**: Fresh install → verify app starts with restored tabs if session exists, settings file shows `restore_session: true`. 2. **Toggle off**: Settings → disable toggle → restart app → verify **single empty 'Untitled' tab** (no previous tabs restored), content empty, no dirty flag (per Task 12). 3. **Toggle on**: Re-enable → restart → verify previous tabs restored correctly (tab count, names, content). 4. **Persistence**: Disable → edit file manually to `false` → restart → stays disabled; set `true` → persists. 5. **Edge cases**: Delete session file → toggle on → starts empty; corrupted JSON → graceful fallback to empty tab (log warning). 6. **Integration**: Combine with Task 12: new empty tab on disable should close cleanly without save prompt.

tasks.title: Fix Linux close button blocked by hit-testing or overlay
tasks.description: Investigate and resolve Linux-specific issue where the window close X button cannot be clicked due to hit-testing failures, overlay interference, or custom title bar rendering problems. Ensure the close button area receives proper click events without invisible elements blocking interaction.
tasks.details: 1. **Identify close button implementation**: Locate window-level close button code in src/main.rs or src/app.rs (search for `Window::new().title_bar(false)` or custom title bar with `egui::TopBottomPanel::top("title_bar")` containing `Button::new("✕").clicked()` to trigger app quit). Check for Linux-specific rendering: `#[cfg(target_os = "linux")]` blocks[5]. 2. **Debug hit-testing**: Add temporary debug overlay in title bar area: `let rect = close_button.rect; ui.painter().rect_filled(rect, 2.0, Color32::RED);` to visualize actual button bounds. Test clicks on Linux (Wayland/X11), verify if clicks register via `ctx.input(|i| i.pointer.button_clicked())` or if consumed by parent frame/drag handler. 3. **Check custom title bar overlaps**: If using custom title bar (common egui template pattern), ensure close button positioned at `ui.allocate_rect(Rect::from_min_size(pos, [24.0,24.0]), Sense::click())` with `min_size` explicitly set. Fix drag interference: wrap title bar drag in `if rect.contains(click_pos) && !close_button_rect.contains(click_pos) { ctx.set_drag_delta(...) }`[5]. 4. **Linux-specific compositor issues**: Test on X11 (`WAYLAND_DISPLAY="" cargo run`) vs Wayland (`cargo run`). Check for transparent overlays or egui memory state interfering with button Ids (`Id::new("window_close")`). Ensure button uses `sense: Sense::click()` not `Sense::drag()`. 5. **Validate Z-order**: Use `ui.painter().rect_stroke(close_rect, 2.0, Stroke::new(3.0, Color32::GREEN))` to confirm rendering order. If blocked by workspace panel (Task 11), adjust panel margins: `SidePanel::left("workspace").resizable(false).show(ctx, |ui| ...)` with explicit spacing.
tasks.testStrategy: 1. **Linux platform verification**: Build/run on Linux (Ubuntu/Fedora, X11+Wayland): `cargo run --target x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu`. Single-click close button → app exits immediately (no multi-click needed). 2. **Hit-test visualization**: Enable debug rects (red/green strokes), verify close button rect visible/clickable, no overlap with drag areas (drag title bar excluding button zone works). 3. **Cross-compositor test**: Wayland (`echo $WAYLAND_DISPLAY`): close works; X11 (`unset WAYLAND_DISPLAY cargo run`): close works. 4. **Interaction regression**: Open workspace panel (post-Task 11), verify panel close X works independently; title bar close unaffected. 5. **Edge cases**: Maximized window, multiple monitors, alt-tab switch → close button responsive. Measure click response <100ms via debug print timestamps.

tasks.title: Add multi-encoding file support with detection and UI controls
tasks.description: Implement automatic encoding detection and conversion for non-UTF-8 files using encoding_rs and chardetng crates, store encoding per tab, display in status bar with click-to-change option, and preserve original encoding on save.
tasks.details: 1. **Add dependencies**: Update Cargo.toml: `encoding_rs = '0.8'`, `chardetng = '3.0'` (or latest stable). Run `cargo update`. 2. **Modify Tab struct**: In src/app.rs or src/tab.rs, add fields: `detected_encoding: Option<String>`, `original_bytes: Vec<u8>`, `current_encoding_label: &'static str` (default 'utf-8'). Use `#[serde(skip_serializing_if = "Option::is_none")]` for persistence. 3. **Implement file loading**: Replace current `std::fs::read_to_string()` with byte-based loading: `let bytes = std::fs::read(&path)?;`. Detect encoding: `let detector = chardetng::detect(&bytes); let encoding = detector.as_encoding().unwrap_or(encoding_rs::UTF_8);`. Decode: `let text = encoding.decode(&bytes).0;`. Store `tab.original_bytes = bytes; tab.detected_encoding = Some(encoding.name().to_string()); tab.current_encoding_label = encoding.name();`. 4. **Re-decode on encoding change**: Add method `fn set_encoding(&mut self, new_label: &'static str) -> Result<(), String> { let encoding = encoding_rs::Encoding::for_label(new_label.as_bytes()).unwrap_or(encoding_rs::UTF_8); self.text = encoding.decode(&self.original_bytes).0; self.current_encoding_label = new_label; Ok(()) }`. 5. **Status bar UI**: In status bar rendering (likely `TopBottomPanel::bottom('status')` or tab footer), add: `ui.horizontal(|ui| { ui.label(format!('{}', tab.current_encoding_label.to_uppercase())); if ui.button('🔄').on_hover_text('Change encoding').clicked() { ui.menu_button('Encoding', |ui| { if ui.button('UTF-8').clicked() { tab.set_encoding('utf-8'); } if ui.button('windows-1252').clicked() { tab.set_encoding('windows-1252'); } if ui.button('shift_jis').clicked() { tab.set_encoding('shift_jis'); } // Add Latin1, ISO-8859-1, etc. }); } });`. 6. **Save with original/preserved encoding**: In save logic: `let encoder = encoding_rs::Encoding::for_label(tab.current_encoding_label.as_bytes()).unwrap(); let encoded = encoder.encode(&tab.text).unwrap(); std::fs::write(&tab.path, encoded)?;`. 7. **Handle BOM**: During detection, strip BOM if present: use `encoding_rs::BOM_SNIFFER.decode(&bytes[..std::cmp::min(3, bytes.len())])` to detect UTF-8/16 BOM and adjust bytes accordingly before chardetng.
tasks.testStrategy: 1. **Unit tests**: Create test file 'latin1.txt' with bytes [72,101,108,108,242] ('Helló' in ISO-8859-1), verify detection returns 'iso-8859-1', decode shows 'Helló', re-encode saves correct bytes. Test Shift-JIS: 'テスト.txt' bytes, verify 'shift_jis' detection and round-trip. 2. **Manual tests**: Save file as Windows-1252 with 'café', open in Ferrite → status shows 'WINDOWS-1252', content correct, click change → switch to UTF-8 → verify text unchanged but re-save uses UTF-8 bytes. 3. **Edge cases**: Empty file → defaults UTF-8, no crash. Pure ASCII file → detects UTF-8.
